chunks.title: Introduction
chunks.content: The slide introduces the historical context of scientists' shift from classical and neo-classical approaches to modeling intelligence, highlighting the emergence of two new fields: connectionism and evolutionary computing. It specifically focuses on evolutionary computing, including genetic algorithms and genetic programming.
chunks.raw_text:
Introduction

▶ After scientists became disillusioned with classical and neo-classical attempts at modeling intelligence, they looked in other directions.

▶ Two prominent fields arose, connectionism (neural networking, parallel processing) and evolutionary computing.

▶ It is the latter that this essay deals with - genetic algorithms and genetic programming.

It specifically focuses on evolutionary computing, including genetic algorithms and genetic programming.

chunks.content: A subset of evolutionary computing that uses techniques inspired by natural selection to generate solutions to optimization problems.

chunks.content: A subset of evolutionary computing that uses techniques inspired by biological evolution to create computer programs.

chunks.title: What is GA
chunks.content: The slide provides an introductory definition of genetic algorithms (GA), explaining their use in computing for optimization and search problems, their categorization as global search heuristics, and their inspiration from evolutionary biology principles.
chunks.raw_text:
What is GA

▶ A genetic algorithm (or GA) is a search technique used in computing to find true or approximate solutions to optimization and search problems.
▶ Genetic algorithms are categorized as global search heuristics.
▶ Genetic algorithms are a particular class of evolutionary algorithms that use techniques inspired by evolutionary biology such as inheritance, mutation, selection, and crossover (also called recombination).

chunks.title: What is GA
chunks.content: The slide provides an introductory explanation of Genetic Algorithms (GA), focusing on their implementation as a computer simulation to solve optimization problems using evolutionary processes. It highlights the representation of solutions and the traditional encoding method.
chunks.raw_text:
What is GA

▶ Genetic algorithms are implemented as a computer simulation in which a population of abstract representations (called chromosomes or the genotype or the genome) of candidate solutions (called individuals, creatures, or phenotypes) to an optimization problem evolves toward better solutions.

▶ Traditionally, solutions are represented in binary as strings of 0s and 1s, but other encodings are also possible.

chunks.title: What is GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the basic concept and process of Genetic Algorithms (GA), focusing on how evolution occurs through generations of randomly generated individuals, fitness evaluation, selection, and modification to form new populations.
chunks.raw_text:
What is GA

▶ The evolution usually starts from a population of randomly generated individuals and happens in generations.

▶ In each generation, the fitness of every individual in the population is evaluated, multiple individuals are selected from the current population (based on their fitness), and modified (recombined and possibly mutated) to form a new population.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the basic concept and process of Genetic Algorithms (GA), focusing on how evolution occurs through generations of randomly generated individuals, fitness evaluation, selection, and modification to form new populations.

chunks.content: A search heuristic that mimics the process of natural evolution. It starts with a population of randomly generated individuals and evolves them over generations through selection, recombination, and mutation.
chunks.embedding_text: Genetic Algorithms (GA): A search heuristic that mimics the process of natural evolution. It starts with a population of randomly generated individuals and evolves them over generations through selection, recombination, and mutation.

chunks.embedding_text: Fitness Evaluation: The process of assessing how well each individual in a population solves a given problem.

chunks.content: Choosing multiple individuals from the current population based on their fitness to reproduce and form a new population.
chunks.embedding_text: Selection: Choosing multiple individuals from the current population based on their fitness to reproduce and form a new population.

chunks.content: Combining genetic information from selected individuals to create new individuals.
chunks.embedding_text: Recombination: Combining genetic information from selected individuals to create new individuals.

chunks.content: Randomly altering parts of the genetic information of individuals to introduce variability.
chunks.embedding_text: Mutation: Randomly altering parts of the genetic information of individuals to introduce variability.

chunks.title: What is GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the iterative process of Genetic Algorithms (GA), focusing on the use of new populations in subsequent iterations, termination conditions, and potential outcomes upon termination.
chunks.raw_text:
What is GA

► The new population is then used in the next iteration of the algorithm.
► Commonly, the algorithm terminates when either a maximum number of generations has been produced, or a satisfactory fitness level has been reached for the population.
► If the algorithm has terminated due to a maximum number of generations, a satisfactory solution may or may not have been reached.

chunks.content: Criteria that stop the algorithm, such as reaching a maximum number of generations or achieving a satisfactory fitness level.

chunks.title: Genotype and Phenotype
chunks.content: The slide provides definitions and distinctions between genotype and phenotype, explaining that genotype refers to the specific set of genes in an organism's genome, while phenotype refers to the observable physical characteristics influenced by the genotype and environment.
chunks.raw_text:
Genotype and Phenotype

▶ Genotype:
- Particular set of genes in a genome

▶ Phenotype:
- Physical characteristic of the genotype (smart, beautiful, healthy, etc.)

chunks.title: GA Requirements
chunks.content: The slide explains the fundamental requirements for implementing a genetic algorithm, focusing on the genetic representation of the solution domain and the fitness function. It also discusses different types of genetic representations and their properties.
chunks.raw_text:
GA Requirements

▶ A typical genetic algorithm requires two things to be defined:
▶ a genetic representation of the solution domain, and
▶ a fitness function to evaluate the solution domain.

▶ A standard representation of the solution is as an array of bits. Arrays of other types and structures can be used in essentially the same way.

▶ The main property that makes these genetic representations convenient is that their parts are easily aligned due to their fixed size, that facilitates simple crossover operation.

▶ Variable length representations may also be used, but crossover implementation is more complex in this case.

▶ Tree-like representations are explored in Genetic programming.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the fundamental requirements for implementing a genetic algorithm, focusing on the genetic representation of the solution domain and the fitness function. It also discusses different types of genetic representations and their properties.

chunks.title: Representation
chunks.content: The slide explains various ways chromosomes can be represented in genetic algorithms, highlighting different data structures and formats.
chunks.raw_text:
Representation

Chromosomes could be:

► Bit strings
								(0101 … 1100)
► Real numbers
						(43.2 -33.1 … 0.0 89.2)
► Permutations of element
			(E11 E3 E7 … E1 E15)
► Lists of rules
				(R1 R2 R3 … R22 R23)
► Program elements
					(genetic programming)
► … any data structure …

chunks.title: GA Requirements
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of fitness functions in Genetic Algorithms (GA), emphasizing their role in measuring solution quality, problem dependency, and specific application in the knapsack problem. It also discusses solution representation, validity, and the use of interactive genetic algorithms when defining fitness expressions is challenging.
chunks.raw_text:
GA Requirements

▶ The fitness function is defined over the genetic representation and measures the quality of the represented solution.

▶ The fitness function is always problem dependent.

▶ For instance, in the knapsack problem we want to maximize the total value of objects that we can put in a knapsack of some fixed capacity.

▶ A representation of a solution might be an array of bits, where each bit represents a different object, and the value of the bit (0 or 1) represents whether or not the object is in the knapsack.

▶ Not every such representation is valid, as the size of objects may exceed the capacity of the knapsack.

▶ The fitness of the solution is the sum of values of all objects in the knapsack if the representation is valid, or 0 otherwise. In some problems, it is hard or even impossible to define the fitness expression; in these cases, interactive genetic algorithms are used.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of fitness functions in Genetic Algorithms (GA), emphasizing their role in measuring solution quality, problem dependency, and specific application in the knapsack problem. It also discusses solution representation, validity, and the use of interactive genetic algorithms when defining fitness expressions is challenging.

chunks.content: A function defined over the genetic representation that measures the quality of the represented solution.
chunks.embedding_text: Fitness Function: A function defined over the genetic representation that measures the quality of the represented solution.

chunks.content: The fitness function is always tailored to the specific problem being solved.
chunks.embedding_text: Problem Dependency: The fitness function is always tailored to the specific problem being solved.

chunks.content: A problem where the goal is to maximize the total value of objects in a knapsack without exceeding its weight capacity.
chunks.embedding_text: Knapsack Problem: A problem where the goal is to maximize the total value of objects in a knapsack without exceeding its weight capacity.

chunks.content: An array of bits where each bit represents whether an object is included (1) or not (0) in the knapsack.

chunks.content: The sum of values of all objects in the knapsack if the representation is valid, otherwise 0.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the initialization step in the general algorithm for Genetic Algorithms (GA). It describes how an initial population of potential solutions is created randomly, covering the entire search space, and occasionally may be seeded in areas where optimal solutions are likely to be found.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ Initialization
▶ Initially many individual solutions are randomly generated to form an initial population. The population size depends on the nature of the problem, but typically contains several hundreds or thousands of possible solutions.
▶ Traditionally, the population is generated randomly, covering the entire range of possible solutions (the search space).
▶ Occasionally, the solutions may be "seeded" in areas where optimal solutions are likely to be found.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the initialization step in the general algorithm for Genetic Algorithms (GA). It describes how an initial population of potential solutions is created randomly, covering the entire search space, and occasionally may be seeded in areas where optimal solutions are likely to be found.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the selection process in Genetic Algorithms (GA), detailing how individuals are chosen based on fitness to breed a new generation. It highlights the fitness-based nature of selection, methods for rating fitness, and the importance of maintaining diversity to avoid premature convergence.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ Selection
▶ During each successive generation, a proportion of the existing population is selected to breed a new generation.
▶ Individual solutions are selected through a fitness-based process, where fitter solutions (as measured by a fitness function) are typically more likely to be selected.
▶ Certain selection methods rate the fitness of each solution and preferentially select the best solutions. Other methods rate only a random sample of the population, as this process may be very time-consuming.
▶ Most functions are stochastic and designed so that a small proportion of less fit solutions are selected. This helps keep the diversity of the population large, preventing premature convergence on poor solutions. Popular and well-studied selection methods include roulette wheel selection and tournament selection.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the selection process in Genetic Algorithms (GA), detailing how individuals are chosen based on fitness to breed a new generation. It highlights the fitness-based nature of selection, methods for rating fitness, and the importance of maintaining diversity to avoid premature convergence.

chunks.embedding_text: Selection: The process of choosing a proportion of the existing population to breed a new generation in Genetic Algorithms.

chunks.content: A method of selecting individual solutions where fitter solutions, measured by a fitness function, are more likely to be chosen.
chunks.embedding_text: Fitness-based process: A method of selecting individual solutions where fitter solutions, measured by a fitness function, are more likely to be chosen.

chunks.content: A function that measures the quality or 'fitness' of individual solutions in a population.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the roulette wheel selection method used in Genetic Algorithms (GA). It describes how individuals are selected based on their fitness proportionate probabilities and how offspring are produced from selected individuals.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ In roulette wheel selection, individuals are given a probability of being selected that is directly proportionate to their fitness.

▶ Two individuals are then chosen randomly based on these probabilities and produce offspring.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the roulette wheel selection method used in Genetic Algorithms (GA). It describes how individuals are selected based on their fitness proportionate probabilities and how offspring are produced from selected individuals.

chunks.content: A measure of how well an individual solution solves the problem; higher fitness increases the chance of selection.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: This slide explains the reproduction step in Genetic Algorithms (GA), detailing how a second generation of solutions is created using genetic operators such as crossover and mutation. It describes the selection of parent solutions, the creation of child solutions, and the iterative process of generating a new population.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ Reproduction
▶ The next step is to generate a second generation population of solutions from those selected through genetic operators: crossover (also called recombination), and/or mutation.
▶ For each new solution to be produced, a pair of "parent" solutions is selected for breeding from the pool selected previously.
▶ By producing a "child" solution using the above methods of crossover and mutation, a new solution is created which typically shares many of the characteristics of its "parents". New parents are selected for each child, and the process continues until a new population of solutions of appropriate size is generated.
chunks.embedding_text: This slide explains the reproduction step in Genetic Algorithms (GA), detailing how a second generation of solutions is created using genetic operators such as crossover and mutation. It describes the selection of parent solutions, the creation of child solutions, and the iterative process of generating a new population.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the outcome of the processes in a Genetic Algorithm (GA), focusing on how the next generation population of chromosomes differs from the initial generation and how the average fitness of the population generally increases through selection and breeding of the best organisms with some inclusion of less fit solutions.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ These processes ultimately result in the next generation population of chromosomes that is different from the initial generation.

▶ Generally the average fitness will have increased by this procedure for the population, since only the best organisms from the first generation are selected for breeding, along with a small proportion of less fit solutions, for reasons already mentioned above.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the outcome of the processes in a Genetic Algorithm (GA), focusing on how the next generation population of chromosomes differs from the initial generation and how the average fitness of the population generally increases through selection and breeding of the best organisms with some inclusion of less fit solutions.

chunks.title: Crossover
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of single point crossover in genetic algorithms, detailing how genetic material from two parents is combined to produce offspring. It highlights the process, probability of occurrence, and visual understanding of chromosome exchange.
chunks.raw_text: the most common type is single point crossover. In single point crossover, you choose a locus at which you swap the remaining alleles from on parent to the other. This is complex and is best understood visually. As you can see, the children take one section of the chromosome from each parent. The point at which the chromosome is broken depends on the randomly selected crossover point. This particular method is called single point crossover because only one crossover point exists. Sometimes only child 1 or child 2 is created, but oftentimes both offspring are created and put into the new population. Crossover does not always occur, however. Sometimes, based on a set probability, no crossover occurs and the parents are copied directly to the new population. The probability of crossover occurring is usually 60% to 70%
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of single point crossover in genetic algorithms, detailing how genetic material from two parents is combined to produce offspring. It highlights the process, probability of occurrence, and visual understanding of chromosome exchange.

chunks.title: Mutation
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of mutation in genetic algorithms, emphasizing its role in introducing genetic diversity after selection and crossover processes. It describes how mutation works by randomly altering alleles with a low probability, ensuring variation within the population.
chunks.raw_text: ▶ After selection and crossover, you now have a new population full of individuals. ▶ Some are directly copied, and others are produced by crossover. ▶ In order to ensure that the individuals are not all exactly the same, you allow for a small chance of mutation. ▶ You loop through all the alleles of all the individuals, and if that allele is selected for mutation, you can either change it by a small amount or replace it with a new value. The probability of mutation is usually between 1 and 2 tenths of a percent. ▶ Mutation is fairly simple. You just change the selected alleles based on what you feel is necessary and move on. Mutation is, however, vital to ensuring genetic diversity within the population.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of mutation in genetic algorithms, emphasizing its role in introducing genetic diversity after selection and crossover processes. It describes how mutation works by randomly altering alleles with a low probability, ensuring variation within the population.

chunks.title: General Algorithm for GA
chunks.content: The slide explains the termination criteria for the general algorithm of Genetic Algorithms (GA). It outlines various conditions under which the generational process of GA is stopped, including finding a satisfactory solution, reaching a fixed number of generations, exhausting allocated resources, plateauing of fitness, manual inspection, or combinations of these conditions.
chunks.raw_text:
General Algorithm for GA

▶ Termination
▶ This generational process is repeated until a termination condition has been reached.
▶ Common terminating conditions are:
▶ A solution is found that satisfies minimum criteria
▶ Fixed number of generations reached
▶ Allocated budget (computation time/money) reached
▶ The highest ranking solution’s fitness is reaching or has reached a plateau such that successive iterations no longer produce better results
▶ Manual inspection
▶ Any Combinations of the above
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the termination criteria for the general algorithm of Genetic Algorithms (GA). It outlines various conditions under which the generational process of GA is stopped, including finding a satisfactory solution, reaching a fixed number of generations, exhausting allocated resources, plateauing of fitness, manual inspection, or combinations of these conditions.

chunks.title: Genetic Programming
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of prefix notation used in programming languages like LISP, highlighting its differences from standard mathematical notation. It provides examples of prefix notation and explains how it simplifies parsing and evaluation by avoiding order precedence issues and parentheses. The slide also mentions the construction of expression trees from these notations.
chunks.raw_text:
Genetic Programming

▶ In programming languages such as LISP, the mathematical notation is not written in standard notation, but in prefix notation. Some examples of this:
▶ + 2 1 : 2 + 1
▶ * + 2 1 2 : 2 * (2+1)
▶ * + - 2 1 4 9 : 9 * ((2 - 1) + 4)
▶ Notice the difference between the left-hand side to the right? Apart from the order being different, no parenthesis! The prefix method makes it a lot easier for programmers and compilers alike, because order precedence is not an issue.
▶ You can build expression trees out of these strings that then can be easily evaluated, for example, here are the trees for the above three expressions.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of prefix notation used in programming languages like LISP, highlighting its differences from standard mathematical notation. It provides examples of prefix notation and explains how it simplifies parsing and evaluation by avoiding order precedence issues and parentheses. The slide also mentions the construction of expression trees from these notations.

chunks.content: A form of mathematical notation where the operator precedes its operands, used in programming languages like LISP.

chunks.title: Genetic Programming
chunks.content: The slide explains the application of Genetic Algorithms (GAs) in evolving expression trees to fit numerical data and answers when no predefined expression exists. It describes the process of 'splicing' and 'grafting' trees, evaluating expressions, and using a fitness function to determine how closely the evolved expression matches the data.
chunks.raw_text:
Genetic Programming

▶ You can see how expression evaluation is thus a lot easier.

▶ What this have to do with GAs? If for example you have numerical data and 'answers', but no expression to conjoin the data with the answers.

▶ A genetic algorithm can be used to 'evolve' an expression tree to create a very close fit to the data.

▶ By 'splicing' and 'grafting' the trees and evaluating the resulting expression with the data and testing it to the answers, the fitness function can return how close the expression is.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the application of Genetic Algorithms (GAs) in evolving expression trees to fit numerical data and answers when no predefined expression exists. It describes the process of 'splicing' and 'grafting' trees, evaluating expressions, and using a fitness function to determine how closely the evolved expression matches the data.

chunks.title: Example
chunks.content: The slide demonstrates an example of using a genetic algorithm to solve a maximization problem by encoding solutions as binary strings, generating an initial population, and evaluating each solution against an objective function.
chunks.raw_text:
Example

▶ f(x) = {MAX(x^2): 0 <= x <= 32 }
▶ Encode Solution: Just use 5 bits (1 or 0).
▶ Generate initial population.

A 0 1 1 0 1
B 1 1 0 0 0
C 0 1 0 0 0
D 1 0 0 1 1

▶ Evaluate each solution against objective.

Sol. String Fitness % of Total
A 01101 169 14.4
B 11000 576 49.2
C 01000 64 5.5
D 10011 361 30.9
chunks.embedding_text: The slide demonstrates an example of using a genetic algorithm to solve a maximization problem by encoding solutions as binary strings, generating an initial population, and evaluating each solution against an objective function.

chunks.title: Example Cont’d
chunks.content: The slide explains the process of generating a new generation of solutions in genetic algorithms, focusing on the selection of parent solutions based on fitness and the methods for creating offspring through reproduction, crossover, and mutation.
chunks.raw_text:
Example Cont’d

► Create next generation of solutions
► Probability of “being a parent” depends on the fitness.

► Ways for parents to create next generation
► Reproduction
► Use a string again unmodified.
► Crossover
► Cut and paste portions of one string to another.
► Mutation
► Randomly flip a bit.
► COMBINATION of all of the above.

chunks.title: Checkboard example
chunks.content: The slide demonstrates a problem of coloring an n by n grid (checkboard) with four colors such that no adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically) share the same color. It shows an initial configuration and a corrected configuration meeting the constraints.
chunks.raw_text:
Checkboard example

► We are given an n by n checkboard in which every field can have a different colour from a set of four colors.
► Goal is to achieve a checkboard in a way that there are no neighbours with the same color (not diagonal)

chunks.title: Checkboard example Cont’d
chunks.content: The slide continues an example involving a graph with n nodes and (n-1) edges, defining the fitness function f(x) for this graph structure.
chunks.raw_text:
Checkboard example Cont’d

• This problem can be seen as a graph with n nodes  and (n-1) edges, so the fitness f(x) is defined as:

f(x) = 2 · (n-1) · n
